is a long, complex structure made of [stars](/wiki/Star "Star") that wrap around the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") [Galaxy](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") in an [orbit](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") that nearly crosses the [galactic poles](/wiki/Galactic_pole "Galactic pole"). It consists of [tidally stripped](/wiki/Tidal_stripping "Tidal stripping") stars from the [Sagittarius Dwarf Elliptical Galaxy](/wiki/Sagittarius_Dwarf_Elliptical_Galaxy "Sagittarius Dwarf Elliptical Galaxy"), resulting from the process of [merging](/wiki/Galaxy_merger "Galaxy merger") with the Milky Way over a period of billions of years.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Long and elongated structure of stars

In [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), the **Sagittarius Stream** is a long, complex structure made of [stars](/wiki/Star "Star") that wrap around the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") [Galaxy](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") in an [orbit](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") that nearly crosses the [galactic poles](/wiki/Galactic_pole "Galactic pole"). It consists of [tidally stripped](/wiki/Tidal_stripping "Tidal stripping") stars from the [Sagittarius Dwarf Elliptical Galaxy](/wiki/Sagittarius_Dwarf_Elliptical_Galaxy "Sagittarius Dwarf Elliptical Galaxy"), resulting from the process of [merging](/wiki/Galaxy_merger "Galaxy merger") with the Milky Way over a period of billions of years.

This stellar stream was originally proposed in 1995 by [Donald Lynden-Bell](/wiki/Donald_Lynden-Bell "Donald Lynden-Bell") after analyzing the distribution of [globular clusters](/wiki/Globular_cluster "Globular cluster") in the Milky Way.[[1]](#cite_note-LB2-1) The actual structure was identified by Newberg and associates (2002)[[2]](#cite_note-Discovery1-2) plus Majewski and associates (2003)[[3]](#cite_note-Discover21-3) using data from the [2MASS](/wiki/2MASS "2MASS") and [SDSS](/wiki/Sloan_Digital_Sky_Survey "Sloan Digital Sky Survey") surveys. In 2006, Belokurov and his collaborators[[4]](#cite_note-Streams-4) found that the Sagittarius Stream has two branches.

When the progenitor object was shredded apart during the interaction, it sent oscillations (analogous to sound waves) through the Milky Way [spiral arm](/wiki/Spiral_arm "Spiral arm") structure. The effects of the oscillations are observed today as layers of alternately denser and sparser [star](/wiki/Star "Star") distributions, above and below the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"). Presently, the position of the Sagittarius Stream relative to the observed layers[[5]](#cite_note-Yanny2013-5) make the Sagittarius Dwarf Elliptical Galaxy the strongest candidate for this intruding object.
